topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy flyer for the relocation package lay crumpled on my desk, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into my meticulously organized life. Liam. Moving to Seattle. The sheer audacity of it made my jaw tighten. I picked up the brochure, my fingers brushing against the glossy paper as if it were contaminated. I watched him across the room, laughing easily with a colleague, his easy confidence a cruel counterpoint to the upheaval he was orchestrating. "Across the country," I muttered under my breath, the sound thin and sharp. I didn't bother to look up when he finally caught my eye, and the brief flicker of apology in his expression only solidified my resolve to ignore him entirely.

[story 2]

I spent an hour staring out the window, watching the familiar skyline shrink behind me. Every time I thought about the logistics—the packing, the new apartment, the inevitable distance—a knot formed in my stomach, tight and unpleasant. It wasn't sadness, exactly; it was more like a physical resistance to the idea. When Liam finally cornered me by the coffee machine, brimming with excitement about his new adventure, I forced a smile that felt brittle on my face. "That sounds… ambitious," I said, my voice flat, deliberately devoid of warmth. I took a slow sip of my lukewarm tea, letting the silence stretch between us until he seemed to sense the shift in the atmosphere, the sudden chill radiating from me.

[story 3]

The news hit me during a late-night video call. Liam’s voice, bright and full of plans, painted a picture of sun-drenched landscapes and new opportunities. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning beneath me, and felt a distinct tightening around my chest. It was an unpleasant sensation, like swallowing sand. I didn't offer a hearty congratulations. Instead, I focused intently on the pattern of the rug beneath my feet, tracing the fibers with my eyes. When he asked if I was okay, I merely shrugged, a small, dismissive movement. "Good for you," I replied, the words clipped and final, already mentally cataloging all the things I wouldn't miss about his departure.
